retrieved_docs.title: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate -- Technique or Treatment -- The Beginning - Getting Started
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient has been catheterized, gentle irrigation of the urethra with a Toomey or bulb syringe should be performed to rinse mucus, blood clots, and other debris into the bladder, where they will not interfere with vision. This will be rinsed out as soon as the resectoscope is positioned inside the bladder.

retrieved_docs.title: Viscoelastics -- Technique or Treatment -- Compartment Technique Using Bimanual Irrigation and Aspiration
retrieved_docs.content: The irrigation and aspiration tip can be moved back and forth to assist the removal of viscoelastic compartments. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Rhinoplasty -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Saline irrigation and bulb syringe

retrieved_docs.title: Dacryocystorhinostomy -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Irrigation of the Lacrimal System
retrieved_docs.content: Advance the cannula into the sac. Determine whether the cannula truly is in the sac by feeling for a hard stop. A soft stop is indicative of a distal canalicular obstruction or, more commonly, a normal kinking of the distal common canaliculus, preventing the cannula passing into the sac.

retrieved_docs.title: Ear Irrigation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Several drops of isopropanol are applied to dry the remaining moisture in the external auditory canal. This step is especially contraindicated if the TM is ruptured.

retrieved_docs.title: Escharotomy -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Marking pen Local anesthetic +/- sedation Sterile preparation, such as chlorhexidine or non-alcoholic povidone-iodine Sterile drapes Scalpel +/- cutting diathermy Diathermy cauterization device Alginate dressing

retrieved_docs.title: Punctal Stenosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Punctal dilation of the stenotic punctum with a Nettleship dilater is performed after the dilater is lubricated with ointment (Fig 5). Diagnostic canalicular probing and nasolacrimal duct irrigation are performed under topical anesthesia. A punctal finder is used to dilate the punctum, and a canalicular probe is inserted. A soft stop indicates canalicular obstruction, and a hard stop against the lacrimal bone indicates a patent canalicular system. Irrigation is performed with a 5ml normal saline syringe. In a normal system, there is the passage of free fluid into the nose or nasopharynx without reflux. Fluorescein dye disappearance after dilation is then performed. The normal flow would show fluorescein disappearance in under 3 minutes. [13]

retrieved_docs.content: Moffett's solution or cocaine drops or paste Chlorhexidine skin preparation solution Turban head drape An endoscope (30 degrees) Light lead Light source stack Endoscrub machine St. Martin forceps Malhotra bone nibbler Kerrison bone punch Neurosurgical patties Crescent knife 15 blade on a long handle Suction freer Blakesley forceps Lacrimal probes Punctum dilator Long dental needle Dental syringe Mini-Monoka or Crawford tubes for nasolacrimal intubation Gelsponge soaked with triamcinolone

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery Interpretation of Technical Problems Impacting Slide Quality -- Issues of Concern -- Staining
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol concentrated at 100% is used for dehydration and additional differentiation. Without additional alcohol baths, the slide may be left with water beads or a haze (see Images. Lacking Additional Alcohol Baths A and B). This can occur when water mixes with the final clearing agent. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Ear Irrigation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Some providers may choose to soften the wax before irrigation. Multiple agents, including mineral oil, 1% sodium docusate solutions, and carbamyl peroxide solutions, may be used. The solutions and water used during the irrigation should be warmed to near body temperature. Cold or hot solutions in the ear are likely uncomfortable for the patient and may make them dizzy or nauseous. If using an IV catheter and syringe, the needle must be removed from the IV catheter.

retrieved_docs.title: Ear Irrigation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The IV catheter is directed superiorly and posteriorly in the ear canal so that the water separates the cerumen from the tympanic membrane. The water stream should not be directed at the TM because this can cause perforation. The injection should not be too rapid, as this may result in trauma, bleeding, and pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Irrigation -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Wound irrigation should not be performed if the wound is actively bleeding, as irrigation may dislodge any clots that are forming. Incomplete wound irrigation can lead to the persistence of debris or purulent discharge left inside the wound, especially in abscesses that may end up in sinus formation. When using povidone-iodine, care must be exercised not to pour it profusely inside the wound however, it should be used on the wound edges.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Central Catheter(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Cleanse the portion of the upper arm with chlorhexidine/alcohol

retrieved_docs.title: Alcohol Sanitizer -- Preparation -- Formulation II
retrieved_docs.content: To make final concentrations of isopropyl alcohol 75% v/v, glycerol 1.45% v/v, hydrogen peroxide 0.125% v/v. Take a 1000 mL graduated flask and add the following Step 1: Add 751.5 mL of 99.8% isopropyl alcohol to the flask Step 2: Add 41.7 mL of 3% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) Step 3: Add 14.5 mL of 98% glycerol Step 4: Top up the flask to the 1000 mL mark with distilled water and gently mix. Additional chemicals that may be present in some formulations include aloe vera, moisturizers, chlorhexidine, triclosan, acids, and other alcohols.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery, Facial Laceration Repair -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Laceration repair requires the following equipment: 1 L or more of sterile saline for irrigation Bulb syringe or 20 to 60 mL capacity catheter-tipped syringe for irrigation Povidone iodine, chlorhexidine, or antiseptic solution of choice Local anesthetic 18- and 27-gauge needles 10 mL syringe Sterile gloves Suction Gauze sponges Castroviejo or Halsey needle driver 0.5-mm Castroviejo or Adson-Brown forceps Mayo or Iris suture scissors Sutures of choice (a selection of 3-0 through 6-0 absorbable and nonabsorbable, monofilament and braided materials, depending on the location of the injury and what structures are involved) Antibiotic ointment or adhesive strip bandage tapes

retrieved_docs.title: Dacryocystorhinostomy -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Irrigation of the Lacrimal System
retrieved_docs.content: Using a lacrimal cannula on a saline-filled syringe (2 ml or 3 ml), place the cannula through the lower punctum and into the mid canalicular area. Syringe and observe for: Passage of saline into the nose (should be graded as a percentage) Reflux via the lower punctum Reflux via the upper punctum Quality of reflux, e.g., mucoid or clear The ease of syringing. The pressure needed to irrigate: this is known as the "educated finger" that the examiner acquires over time.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: highlighting alcohol as a major risk factor for variceal development. Therapeutic endoscopic interventions are organized under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء (esophageal therapeutic procedures). Esophagoscopy for control of hemorrhage and esophagoscopy for injection of esophageal varices are classified as procedures on the esophagus and are related to non‑upper‑GI therapeutic procedures; both are linked to دوالي المَريء and وريد متدلي. Specific esophageal therapeutic procedures include ربط دوالي المريء (esophageal variceal ligation) and مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal stenting). In the context of upper GI treatment, non‑incisional irrigation, cleaning and local instillation of the digestive system is a parent procedure that encompasses غسل المعدة (gastric lavage), which is recognized as a non‑operative approach for managing upper GI conditions. The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with chest radiography (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر), emphasizing the role of imaging in the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 212
retrieved_docs.content: of both esophageal and gastric varices. Clinical associations connect alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول) and alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) with hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي), which in turn is associated with esophageal variceal bleeding. Alcohol use is also clinically linked to intestinal varices and to traumatic injuries such as closed head injury (إصابة الرأس المغلقة), ankle fracture (كسر الكاحل), and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة). Therapeutic interventions for these conditions include non‑incisional irrigation, cleaning and local instillation of the digestive system (e.g., gastric lavage غسل المعدة) and other non‑OR upper GI therapeutic procedures, which encompass procedures such as عملية في المريء performed during operations on esophageal varices. Additional esophageal therapeutic actions comprise ربط دوالي المريء (esophageal variceal ligation) and مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal stenting). retrieved_docs.content: irrigating solutions and electrolyte‑balancing formulations are organized within a unified pharmacological framework, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous therapies for fluid resuscitation, nutrition, electrolyte management, antifibrinolytic treatment and metabolic modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: procedures, gastric tube use, gastric lavage, venostomy, and related research applications—under broader clinical and procedural categories to guide comprehensive patient care and scientific investigation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 100
retrieved_docs.content: ingredients, and therapeutic categories, thereby assisting clinicians in selecting appropriate formulations for fluid replacement, electrolyte correction, anticoagulation, antifibrinolysis, and related therapeutic goals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 909
retrieved_docs.content: Non‑incisional irrigation, cleaning and local instillation in the digestive system encompasses the procedure known as غسل المعدة (gastric lavage). Gastric lavage is clinically associated with نَزْف مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ (gastrointestinal hemorrhage). Calcium gluceptate (calcium gluconate) may be used to prevent نَزْف مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ, offering a potential protective strategy against the bleeding complications linked to gastric lavage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1463
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfective irrigating solutions are classified as members of the nitrofurazone group, indicating that these irrigation preparations incorporate nitrofurazone as their active anti‑infective component and are used for antimicrobial cleansing in clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2784
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfective irrigating solutions are classified within the broader category of antiinfective ophthalmologics, indicating that they are a specific type of eye medication used to prevent or treat ocular infections through irrigation. This broader relationship places antiinfective irrigating solutions among the various antiinfective ophthalmologics that are employed in ophthalmic care to manage infectious conditions of the eye, highlighting their role in the overall therapeutic arsenal for ocular infection control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2652
retrieved_docs.content: Gastric lavage, a procedure employed to cleanse the stomach, is clinically associated with secondary gastrointestinal bleeding, indicating that this intervention can be linked to or may precipitate bleeding within the gastrointestinal tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1251
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Non‑incisional irrigation, cleaning and local instillation, digestive system” includes the procedure referred to as “غسل المعدة” (gastric lavage). Gastric lavage is clinically associated with “نَزْف مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ” (secondary gastrointestinal hemorrhage), indicating that while this non‑surgical stomach‑cleansing technique can be useful, it carries a risk of causing or being linked to secondary GI bleeding. Recognizing this relationship is important for clinicians when considering gastric lavage and for monitoring patients for potential hemorrhagic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: same parent structure. Overall, the knowledge base connects vascular diseases of veins and lymphatics with esophageal and gastric variceal pathology, links alcohol‑related liver injury to esophageal bleeding, and integrates endoscopic, non‑surgical, and operative therapeutic approaches—including esophageal ligation, sclerotherapy, gastric lavage, and hemorrhoid surgery—in the management of related gastrointestinal complications.
